coredata.dc:title: Manganese dioxide nanosheet-modulated ratiometric fluoroprobe based on carbon quantum dots from okra for selective and sensitive dichlorvos detection in foods
coredata.dc:creator: Mei, He
Zhu, Xiaolei
Li, Ziqian
Jiang, Jiahui
Wang, Huili
Wang, Xuedong
Zhou, Peipei
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we developed a ratiometric fluoroprobe by integrating okra-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with amplex red (AR) using manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) as a medium. Fluorescence intensities (FIs) of CQDs were sharply quenched by MnO2 NSs via an inner-filter effect processes, whereas the FIs of AR were significantly enhanced due to oxidation of AR to AR-ox by the oxidase-mimetic activity of MnO2 NSs. Acetyrylcholinesterase hydrolyzed acetylthiocholine to produce thiocholine, and the decomposition of MnO2 NSs to Mn2+ by thiocholine led to the FI recovery of CQDs, but decreased FIs of AR-ox. Based on the above phenomenon and the inhibitory effect of dichlorvos (DDVP) on acetyrylcholinesterase activity, a novel ratiometric fluoroprobe for DDVP quantification was pioneered. Under optimized conditions, this fluoroprobe gave a wide linear range (4–120 μg/L), low detection limit (1.2 μg/L), and satisfactory fortification recoveries (90.0–110.0%), thereby providing good prospects for routine DDVP monitoring in foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluoroprobe
Okra-derived carbon quantum dots
Amplex red
Manganese dioxide nanosheets
Dichlorvos

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CQDs synthesis using okra as a carbon precursor
Measurement of quantum yields (QY) for CQDs
Construction of ratiometric fluoroprobe based on the MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR mixed system
DDVP detection by the as-constructed ratiometric fluoroprobe
DDVP detection in real-world food samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was jointly sponsored by the Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22076134 ), Key Science & Technology Project of Suzhou City (SS202028 and 2022SS24), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation ( LQ21B050004 ) and South Zhejiang Institute of Radiation Medicine and Nuclear Technology (ZFY-2022-K-004).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HR-TEM image of CQDs, Insert: the diameter distribution of CQDs; (B) XPS spectra of CQDs; (C) FT-IR spectra of CQDs; (D) Fluorescent excitation (Ex) and fluorescent emission (Em) spectra of CQDs, Insert: photographs of CQDs under daylight (photograph I) and UV light at 365 nm (photograph II).
(A) HR-TEM image of MnO2 NSs; (B) AFM image of MnO2 NSs; (C) XPS spectra of MnO2 NSs; (D) UV–vis absorption of MnO2 NSs, Insert: photographs of MnO2 NSs under daylight.

(A) Fluorescent emission spectra of CQDs-AR (a: red line) and fluorescent emission spectra of CQDs-AR in the presence of MnO2 NSs (b: black line); (B) Fluorescent excitation spectra of CQDs (Ex: black line) and fluorescent emission spectra of CQDs (Em: red line), and UV–Vis absorption spectra of MnO2 NSs (Abs: blue line); (C) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of CQDs without (a: red line) and with (b: blue line) MnO2 NSs; (D) UV–Vis absorption spectra of MnO2 NSs without (a: black line) and with (b: red line) TMB.

(A) Fluorescent emission spectra of CQDs/AR (a) and MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR (b) with ATCh (c), AChE (d), AChE/ATCh (e) and AChE/ATCh/DDVP (f); (B) Fluorescent emission spectra of MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR/AChE/ATCh with different concentrations of DDVP; (C) The calibration plot of DDVP determined by the fluorescent platform based on MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR; (D) The selectivity of the fluorescent platform based on MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR in the presence of various interference substances (a: DDVP (20 μg/L), b: Nitenpyram, c: Carbendazim, d: Dinotefuran, e: Dimethoate, f: 2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic acid, g: Glufosinate ammonium, h: Deltamethrin (100 μg/L).
Schematic illustration of ratiometric fluoroprobe based on MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR for DDVP detection.
Analytical results of the fluorescent platform based on MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR for DDVP detection in fruit juices.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The fortified “Found” concentration is equal to the actually detected value minus the blank value (unfortified sample).
ND. represents the acronym of non-detectable.
RSDs (%) are achieved from the tests in triplicate.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical performance of the as-constructed fluorescent platform based on MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR for DDVP detection in fruits and vegetables.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The fortified “Found” concentration is equal to the actually detected value minus the blank value (unfortified sample).
ND. represents the acronym of non-detectable.
RSDs (%) are achieved from the tests in triplicate.

head.ce:title: Manganese dioxide nanosheet-modulated ratiometric fluoroprobe based on carbon quantum dots from okra for selective and sensitive dichlorvos detection in foods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhejiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Watershed Science and Health, College of Public Health and Management, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhejiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Watershed Science and Health
College of Public Health and Management

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: South Zhejiang Institute of Radiation Medicine and Nuclear Technology, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325014, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: South Zhejiang Institute of Radiation Medicine and Nuclear Technology, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325014, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou 215009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering
Suzhou University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou 215009, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: He Mei and Xiaolei Zhu contributed equally to this work and share first authorship.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of ratiometric fluoroprobe based on MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR for DDVP detection.

Herein, we developed a ratiometric fluoroprobe by integrating okra-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with amplex red (AR) using manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) as a medium. Fluorescence intensities (FIs) of CQDs were sharply quenched by MnO2 NSs via an inner-filter effect processes, whereas the FIs of AR were significantly enhanced due to oxidation of AR to AR-ox by the oxidase-mimetic activity of MnO2 NSs. Acetyrylcholinesterase hydrolyzed acetylthiocholine to produce thiocholine, and the decomposition of MnO2 NSs to Mn2+ by thiocholine led to the FI recovery of CQDs, but decreased FIs of AR-ox. Based on the above phenomenon and the inhibitory effect of dichlorvos (DDVP) on acetyrylcholinesterase activity, a novel ratiometric fluoroprobe for DDVP quantification was pioneered. Under optimized conditions, this fluoroprobe gave a wide linear range (4–120 μg/L), low detection limit (1.2 μg/L), and satisfactory fortification recoveries (90.0–110.0%), thereby providing good prospects for routine DDVP monitoring in foods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dichlorvos (DDVP) is an organophosphate pesticide (OP) that is widely applied as an insecticide in modern agriculture (Yan et al., 2015). The extensive use of DDVP has led to the widespread accumulation of residues in various plants, food and environmental media. Thus, it is imperative to develop simple and sensitive methods for the quick detection of trace-level DDVP residues. To date, many analytical approaches have been used to detect DDVP residues, including high-performance liquid chromatography, gas chromatography, electrochemical method, colorimetry, and fluorescent assays. Among these approaches, fluorescent assays have gained extensive interest due to their favorable sensitivity, selectivity, rapid response, non-destructive attributes, and simple operation (Rong et al., 2015). Therefore, researchers have devoted great efforts to design and develop many types of fluorescent assays for quantification of DDVP residues.

As a class of two-dimensional nanomaterials, MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) exhibit strong absorption intensity over the wavelength range of 200–600 nm (Na et al., 2018). Thus, MnO2 NSs are often utilized as an efficient broad-spectrum quencher or energy acceptor and integrated with a variety of fluorescent nanomaterials for designing efficient fluoroprobes, including carbon quantum dots (CQDs), boron nitride quantum dots and copper nanoclusters. For example, He et al. (2015) designed a sensitive turn-on fluoroprobe for glutathione using single-layer MnO2 NSs as a quencher and recognition unit, as well as N-doped CQDs as a reporter. Similarly, Na et al. (2018) integrated reduced graphene quantum dots with MnO2 NSs to establish an “OFF-ON” fluoroprobe for alkaline phosphatase. A fluorescent “turn-off” sensing platform for paraoxon was developed based on CQDs, in which MnO2 NSs functioned as a reporter and dual-functional quencher and recognizer (Yan et al., 2018). Moreover, Mandal et al. (2019) fabricated CQDs with amine, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of gelatin, and used them to develop an “ON-OFF” sensing platform for chlorpyrifos. Of these fluorescent nanomaterials, CQDs possess many distinctive merits, including strong photometric stability, tunable fluorescence, favorable water solubility, and biological compatibility.

Most previous applications of CQDs employed carbon-rich chemicals as precursors, which can pose an environment risk, thereby limiting their practical application (Ensafi et al., 2017). In recent years, CQDs have been fabricated from natural carbon precursors due to their ease of access, recycling of waste biomass and environmental friendliness. A variety of green materials have been used as natural carbon precursors for the synthesis of CQDs. A CQDs-based turn-on fluoroprobe was pioneered from the burned ash of wastepaper as carbon precursors for OP detection (Lin et al., 2018). Highly-fluorescence CQDs from tomato fruit extract were employed for determining diazinon with a favorable efficiency (Khaledian et al., 2021). In addition, heteroatom doping is widely used to further enhance the fluorescent properties of CQDs (Wan et al., 2019; Bandi et al., 2018).

Natural carbon precursors possess a wide variety of chemical components with abundant heteroatoms, which are conducive to developing CQDs with favorable properties for intrinsic heteroatom doping. Okra is widely grown in tropical, subtropical and warm regions, and its juice contains a large amount of protein, fat, carbohydrates, vitamins and fiber, as well as numerous heteroatoms (calcium, phosphorus, iron, potassium and manganese) (Yang et al., 2023; Raju et al., 2012). Informed by previous studies, we fabricated doped/modified CQDs using okra as a carbon precursor without complex synthetic processes or additional chemical reagents, which was anticipated to improve the fluorescent properties of the CQDs. To date, there are no reports of fluorescent sensors based on MnO2 NSs mediated CQDs using okra as a natural carbon precursor.

Previously reported fluoroprobes were mainly based on a single wavelength emission, which can be affected by several environmental factors (Gu et al., 2017). In contrast, ratiometric (dual-emitting) fluoroprobes, based on the ratio of two well-resolved emissions, can appreciably improve sensitivity and accuracy through a self-calibration system (Huang et al., 2018). In particular, ratiometric fluorescence sensors, based on opposing responses to target analytes, are highly preferred. MnO2 NSs with oxidase-like activity have been used as a mediator to construct ratiometric fluorescent sensors with high sensitivity (Fan et al., 2017). An organic pigment pair for a ratiometric fluoroprobe was developed based on scopoletin (SC) and amplex red (AR) for OP detection (Yao et al., 2019). OPs significantly suppressed the activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and prevent the decomposition of MnO2 NSs resulting in a simultaneous decrease in the fluorescence intensity (FI) of SC and an increase in the FI of AR. As a typical fluoroprobe, nonfluorescent AR can be converted to oxidized AR (AR-ox) having a strong red fluorescence due to the oxidization of MnO2 NSs. Thus, we sought to develop a MnO2 NSs mediated ratiometric fluorescent platform based on AR and okra-derived CQDs for DDVP detection.

The FIs of okra-derived CQDs were prominently quenched by MnO2 NSs via an inner filter effect (IFE) process, whereas the FI of AR was enhanced due to the oxidation reaction between AR and MnO2 NSs (Scheme 1 ). Thiocholine (TCh) resulting from the hydrolytic reaction between AChE and acetylthiocholine (ATCh) triggered the decomposition of MnO2 NSs to Mn2+. Thus, the appearance of TCh resulted in the prominent recovery of the CQD FIs versus a prominent decrease in the FI of AR. The addition of DDVP suppresses the activity of AChE and promotes the generation of TCh, thereby causing opposing fluorescent responses from CQDs and AR. The resulting analytical platform for DDVP detection demonstrates that the MnO2 NSs-mediated ratiometric fluoroprobe has excellent prospects for DDVP quantification in food matrices.

Four standard chemicals were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China): ATCh (99.0%), AChE (99.0%), 2-morpholinoethanesulfonic acid (MES, 99.0%), and AR (98.0%). The following chemicals with purities > 98.0% were sourced from Aladdin (Shanghai, China): 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), DDVP, nitenpyram, carbendazim, dinotefuran, dimethoate, 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid, glufosinate ammonium, and deltamethrin. Ultrapure water (>18.2 MΩ) was produced by a Milli-Q gradient system (Bedford, MA, USA). Okra, juices, fruits, and vegetables were purchased from a local market (Wenzhou, China).

HR-TEM images of the CQDs and MnO2 NSs were acquired on a Talos F200S (Thermo Scientific, NY, USA). FT-IR spectra of nanomaterials were collected on a Bruker Tensor II spectrometer (Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the surface composition of CQDs (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was conducted on a Dimension Icon (Bruker, Germany). UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs and MnO2 NSs were obtained on a PERSEE TU-1901 (Pgeneral, China). The FIs of all tested solutions were determined on a FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, England). GC–MS was performed on an Agilent Technologies 7890B GC System (USA) equipped with J&W DB-1701 column (30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm) and Agilent Technologies 5977A MSD (USA) equipped with an EI source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs synthesis using okra as a carbon precursor
ce:section.ce:para: Using okra as a carbon precursor, CQDs were fabricated based on a facile hydrothermal approach (Qu et al., 2017). Briefly, an aliquot (5.5 g) of the fresh okra sample was washed with ultrapure water, cut into pieces after air drying at room temperature, and homogenized by grinding. Subsequently, 75 mL ultrapure water was added to the ground okra and stirred for 20 min. The mixture was transferred into a 100-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 7 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction mixture was filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane filter to remove particulate matter and stored at 4 °C for subsequent trials.

According to the method reported by Yan et al. (2018), 1 mL of KMnO4 solution with varying concentrations was added to 2.5 mL of MES buffer (0.1 M, pH 6) and diluted to 10 mL with ultrapure water. After vortexing, the mixed solution was ultrasonicated until a brown colloidal solution formed, which was stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of quantum yields (QY) for CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Quinine sulfate solution in 0.1 M H2SO4 was adopted as a standard (QYR = 0.54) and the QY of the CQDs was determined (Shi et al., 2016). To reduce self-absorption, the optical densities (ODs) of CQDs and quinine sulfate were adjusted to < 0.1 and the QY was calculated based on the following equation:
QY X
=
QY R
×
I X
I R
×
A R
A X
×
η X
η R
2
where “I” is the integrated FI; “A” is OD at the excitation wavelength; “η” represents the solvent refractive index; and subscripts “X” and “R” refer to CQDs and quinine sulfate, respectively.

After DDVP was added to the AChE solution (50 μL, 500 U/L), the mixed system was incubated for 10 min at room temperature. Subsequently, 50 μL of ATCh (0.7 mM) was fortified into the mixed system and incubated for 25 min at 35 °C. After that, 50 μL of CQDs, 50 μL of AR (32 mM) and 500 μL of MnO2 NSs were successively added to the reaction solution and quantitatively diluted to a final volume of 1.25 mL with Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.4). After a 10-min incubation, fluorescence spectra were acquired for the DDVP assay.

Juice samples were filtered through 0.22-µm membrane filters to remove insoluble substances, and the filtrate preserved for DDVP detection. To determine the fortified recovery of the as-constructed ratiometric fluoroprobe, the filtrate was fortified with a standard solution of DDVP at 25, 50 and 100 μg/L. For the detection of DDVP in fruit and vegetables, the apple, strawberry, celery, tomato and okra samples were ground into homogenates. Aliquots (20 g) of the homogenates were mixed with 40 mL acetonitrile to form suspensions, and 5 g of NaCl was added to the suspension and mixed for 1 min. Then, the suspension was centrifuged at 3,000 rpm for 5 min to collect the supernatant. All purification and detection procedures were conducted according to China standard method (GB 23200.8–2016). To evaluate the fortified recovery, 0.1, 0.2 and 1.0 μg/g of DDVP were spiked into the supernatant, which was then diluted 20 times using ultrapure water. Finally, 50 μL of the spiked solution was incubated with AChE solution (50 μL, 500 U/L) for 10 min, and subsequently 50 μL of ATCh (0.7 mM) was added to the above solution. After incubation, 50 μL of CQDs, 50 μL of AR (32 mM), and 500 μL of MnO2 NSs were sequentially added to the reaction solution and finally diluted to 1.25 mL with Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.4). To determine the concentrations of DDVP, the fluorescence spectra of the mixed systems were measured after a 10-min incubation.

HR-TEM images of the as-synthesized CQDs showed a size range of 1.2–3.0 nm with an average size of 2.1 nm; no aggregation phenomenon was observed indicating good dispersion (Fig. 1 A). These observations were in general agreement with previous studies (Xu et al., 2014; Li et al., 2018). XPS analysis examined the chemical components of CQDs (Fig. 1B). Four peaks at 285.9, 400.4, 533.4 and 1071.7 eV were attributable to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Na 1 s, respectively (Mei et al., 2022). Both N and Na have the potential to change the electronic structure of CQDs and regulate their fluorescence properties via synergic action (Fang et al., 2015). The HR-XPS spectrum of C1s displayed four subpeaks at 284.5, 285.6, 286.0 and 287.7 eV (Fig. S1A), which were attributable to CC/CC, CN, CO and CO bonds, respectively (Li et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019). By way of contrast, the O 1 s spectrum displayed two major subpeaks at 530.9 and 531.9 eV (Fig. S1B), highlighting the presence of CO and C-OH/COC groups in the CQDs (Shi et al., 2016). As for the N1s spectrum (Fig. S1C), the three main peaks at 399.8, 400.8 and 401.6 eV belonged to CN (CN) and NH, respectively (Wan et al., 2019; Bandi et al., 2018).

To further analyze the surface functional groups of the CQDs, FT-IR was employed to analyze the chemical components of CQDs. As displayed in Fig. 1C, the peak located at 3210 cm−1 belonged to the vibrational stretching of OH/NH, and those at 2978, 2929 and 2869 cm−1 were attributable to the CH vibration of the methylene group (Chaudhry et al., 2020). The peaks at 1772 and 1672 cm−1 were identified as the stretching vibrations of CO. The peaks at 1597, 1392 and 1048 cm−1 were ascribed to CC bending, CH bending and CO stretching, respectively. The peaks at 1277 and 875 cm−1 resulted from CO/CN stretching vibrations and CH/NH2 out-of-plane bending vibration, respectively (Bhatt et al., 2018). These spectral data confirm the presence of several functional groups, such as COOH, OH, CO, and NH2, on the surface of the okra-derived CQDs.

When the mixed system was excited from 300 to 380 nm, the fluorescent emission peaks shifted to longer wavelengths (Fig. S1D), implying that the okra-derived CQDs displayed an excitation-dependent mode. The highest fluorescent emission was observed at 400 nm when the mixed system was excited at 330 nm. As such, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of CQDs were located at 330 and 400 nm, respectively (Fig. 1D). As shown in Fig. 1D, the CQDs displayed transparent and yellowish solutions under daylight light (photograph I), whereas they displayed a bright blue fluorescence under a 365-nm UV lamp (photograph II).

Hydrothermal temperature and time often have significant effects on the fluorescent properties of okra-derived CQDs (Ogi et al., 2016). To achieve CQDs with the highest QY, we optimized these two variables using a one-factor-at-a-time method. As shown in Fig. S2 A, the FIs of CQDs increased with increasing temperatures and reached a plateau at 200 °C. The hydrothermal time had only a slight effect on the FIs of CQDs (Fig. S2B). In summary, the optimized reaction temperature and time were 200 ℃ and 7 h, respectively. Under optimized conditions, the QY of the CQDs reached as high as 18.6%, which is sufficient to meet the requirements for most sensing applications (Thulasi et al., 2020). As for the stability of CQDs, the FIs of CQDs maintained 97.0% of their initial FI at 4 °C after three weeks of storage (Fig. S3), demonstrating their high photo-stability.

HR-TEM observation revealed that MnO2 NSs exhibited an ultrathin flake structure (Fig. 2A). The thickness of MnO2 NSs was estimated to be ∼ 5.2 nm according to their AFM images (Fig. 2B). XPS analysis showed five main peaks (166.3, 231.3, 285.9, 401.8 and 530.8 eV) that were ascribed to S 2p, S 2 s, C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively, while the two peaks at 640.0 and 652.8 eV were attributed to Mn 2p (Fig. 2C). These findings confirm that the MnO2 NSs were mainly composed of S, C, N, O and Mn, while the S, C and N originated from the MES buffer. HR-XPS identified two distinct peaks at 653.5 and 642.0 eV that are related to Mn2p1/2 and Mn2p3/2 in the MnO2, respectively (Fig. S4), as previously report by Wu et al. (2018). The UV–vis absorption of MnO2 NSs showed a wide absorption band across the range of 200–600 nm, and a strong absorption peak at ∼ 385 nm (Fig. 2D). The color of the MnO2 NSs was brown under daylight light (Fig. 2D-insert). Overall, these characterization results confirm that MnO2 NSs were successfully fabricated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of ratiometric fluoroprobe based on the MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR mixed system
ce:section.ce:para: The novel ratiometric fluoroprobe was composed of CQDs, AR and MnO2 NSs, in which CQDs and AR functioned as the fluorescent reporter and MnO2 NSs as a dual-functional fluorescent quencher and recognizer. The FI of CQDs at 400 nm was ∼ 2.1 × 105 a.u., while that of AR at 585 nm was negligible in the absence of MnO2 NSs (Fig. 3 A). The addition of MnO2 NSs resulted in an appreciably decrease in the FI of CQDs, as low as ∼ 3.1 × 104 a.u.. Conversely, the 585-nm FI of AR was sharply enhanced to 2.2 × 105 a.u. by MnO2 NSs addition. These phenomena indicate that MnO2 NSs prominently quench the fluorescence of CQDs and promote the oxidation of AR to AR-ox generating a red fluorescence.

To confirm the underlying mechanisms regarding the quenching process of MnO2 NSs toward CQDs, the UV–vis spectrum of MnO2 and fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of CQDs were measured. The UV–vis absorption of MnO2 displayed considerable overlap with the fluorescent excitation/emission spectra of CQDs (Fig. 3B), implying that the fluorescence quenching process of MnO2 NSs toward CQDs originates from fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or IFE (Xu et al., 2018). The fluorescence decay curves for the CQDs (Fig. 3C) were used to calculate an average fluorescence lifetime of 5.31 ns for the CQDs, which remained nearly constant in the presence of MnO2 NSs (τavg = 5.21 ns). These data suggest that the quenching of CQDs by MnO2 NSs was due to an IFE process rather than a FRET process because the fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs would be expected to decrease in an efficient FRET process (Zhang et al., 2018).

To further explore the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CQDs by MnO2 NSs, the zeta potentials of MnO2 NSs, CQDs and MnO2 NSs/CQDs were assessed. The zeta potentials of MnO2 NSs and CQDs were −15.85 and −11.85 mV, respectively (Fig. S5), which are consistent with previously reported data (He et al., 2015; Yan et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021). Compared to the CQDs alone, the zeta potential of the mixed MnO2 NSs/CQDs decreased to as low as −27.1 mV due to the presence of MnO2 NSs. These findings indicate that the surfaces of MnO2 NSs and CQDs were both negatively charged, with an electrostatic repulsion distance between them of > 10 nm. Consequently, the fluorescence quenching of CQDs by MnO2 NSs was not attributable to a FRET process because the distance between CQDs and MnO2 NSs would be in the range of 1 to 10 nm in an efficient FRET process (Zu et al., 2017; Xie et al., 2018). These analyses led us to posit that the fluorescence quenching of CQDs by MnO2 NSs resulted from an IFE phenomenon.

TMB is widely utilized as a fluoroprobe to verify the occurrence of oxidase-like activities in nanomaterials because it is catalyzed to generate a blue oxidized product (TMBox) (Yan et al., 2017). No clear absorption peak appeared in the absence of TMB, whereas a strong 656-nm peak occurred in the presence of TMB (Fig. 3D), demonstrating that the MnO2 NSs possessed oxidase-like activity. Thus, it can be posited that AR was oxidated to produce AR-ox owing to the oxidase-like activity of MnO2 NSs. In sum, the fluorescence of CQDs was quenched by MnO2 NSs due to IFE phenomenon, and AR was oxidized to AR-ox having strong fluorescence owing to the oxidase-like activity of MnO2 NSs. Based on these experimental phenomena, the CQDs/AR was deemed appropriate for constructing a ratiometric fluoroprobe when MnO2 NSs were added as a medium.

To optimize the performance of the CQDs/AR based fluoroprobe, the concentration of KMnO4 and buffer solution pH were assessed. The FIs of CQDs decreased as the KMnO4 concentration increased from 3.0 to 6.5 mM, with no obvious variation from 6.5 to 7.5 mM (Fig. S6A). This indicated that the quenching efficiency reached a maximum when the KMnO4 concentration was 6.5 mM. The FIs of AR-ox increased with increasing KMnO4 concentration from 3.0 to 7.0 mM, with a slight decreased with further increases from 7.0 to 7.5 mM. This demonstrates that AR was completely oxidized to AR-ox by MnO2 NSs. The ratios of I585 to I400 (I585/I400, I585 and I400 represented the 585-nm FIs of AR-ox and 400-nm FIs of CQDs, respectively) increased with increasing KMnO4 concentration from 3.0 to 6.5 mM. However, as the concentration of KMnO4 increase from 6.5 to 7.5 mM, there was a slight decrease in I585/I400. Consequently, the optimized KMnO4 concentration was set at 6.5 mM.

The I585/I400 value progressively increased as the buffer solution pH increased from 3.4 to 8.4, but then decreased as pH further increased from 8.4 to 9.4 (Fig. S6B). According to the previous reports (Chen et al., 2014; Kim et al., 2020; Li et al., 2019), MnO2 NSs become unstable in acidic solutions, AR becomes unstable at solution pH > 8.5 and AR-ox may be further oxidized to weakly fluorescent resazurin at lower pH values. The highest activity of AChE was found within the buffer pH range of 8.0 to 9.0 (Hou et al., 2015). Thus, a buffer pH of 8.4 was selected to optimize the sensitivity of the ratiometric fluoroprobe for DDVP detection. For a practical applications, the stability of the ratiometric fluoroprobe based on the MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR system is particularly important. The I585/I400 decreased by < 4.5% after three weeks of storage at 4 °C, demonstrating excellent stability of the newly developed ratiometric fluoroprobe (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: DDVP detection by the as-constructed ratiometric fluoroprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 presents the basic principle of the ratiometric fluoroprobe for DDVP detection based on MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR. Firstly, TCh is produced from the specific reaction: ATCh + AChE = HAc + TCh. Secondly, TCh reduces MnO2 NSs to Mn2+. Thirdly, DDVP suppresses the activity of AChE, thereby hindering the decomposition of MnO2 NSs and influencing the fluorescent behavior of the newly developed fluoroprobe. Hence, MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR was expected to provide a sensitive ratiometric fluoroprobe for DDVP detection. In fact, the FIs of CQDs were appreciably quenched, whereas those of AR-ox were remarkably enhanced when MnO2 NSs were added to the mixed system (Fig. 4 A). In the absence of ATCh or AChE, the FIs of CQDs and AR-ox were the same as those of the mixed MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR system. When ATCh and AChE were added to the MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR system, the FI of CQDs prominently increased whereas the FI of AR-ox markedly decreased. When DDVP was added to the MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR/ATCh/AChE system, AChE activity was suppressed and little MnO2 NS was reduced resulting in a weaker fluorescent signal at 400-nm for CQDs and a stronger signal at 585-nm for AR-ox. These results demonstrate the efficacy of the MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR as a ratiometric fluoroprobe for DDVP detection.

To maximize the analytical performance, several reaction variables were rigorously optimized, including AChE and ATCh concentrations and the reaction time and temperature between AChE and DDVP. The I585/I400 value sharply decreased from 2.74 to 0.23 with increasing ATCh concentrations (0–0.45 mM), and then remained nearly constant at a level of ∼ 0.23 (Fig. S8A). Consequently, the optimized concentration of ATCh was set at 0.45 mM in subsequent trials. Further, the I585/I400 value decreased rapidly before approaching a minimum at a AChE level of 60 U L−1 (Fig. S8B). This indicates that the ATCh (0.45 mM) was completely hydrolyzed as the AChE concentration reached 60 U L−1. Therefore, 60 U L−1 of AChE was used throughout the subsequent optimization experiments. The inhibitory effect of DDVP on AChE reached a maximum at 25 min of incubation, and the enzymatic reaction exhibited its highest catalytic efficiency at 35 °C (Fig. S8C and Fig. S8D). Thus, 25 min and 35 °C were selected as the optimized reaction time and temperature for AChE and DDVP reactions.

To investigate the analytical performance of the developed fluoroprobe, the fluorescent emission spectra of the mixed system were evaluated at varying DDVP concentrations under optimized conditions. When DDVP concentrations increased from 0 to 120 μg/L, the FIs of CQDs monotonically decreased, whereas those of AR-ox progressively increased (Fig. 4B). A plot of I585/I400 values vs. DDVP concentrations produced a relationship with a the linear range (LR) of 4–120 μg/L and determination coefficient (R2) of 0.9970 (Fig. 4C). Correspondingly, the limit of detection (LOD) for DDVP was calculated as 1.2 μg/L Based on a signal to noise ratio of 3 (S/N = 3), which was ∼ 8-fold lower than the maximum regulatory residual level of 10 μg/L (Chen et al., 2020). The analytical performance of the ratiometric fluoroprobe, in terms of LR and LOD, was comparable or better relative to other colorimetric, electrochemical and fluorescent sensors (Table S1). These comparisons demonstrate that the MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR system is highly conducive to constructing a sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensor for DDVP detection.

The selectivity of the ratiometric fluoroprobe was evaluated when other pesticides (nitenpyram, carbendazim, dinotefuran, dimethoate, 2,4-dicholorphynoxyacetic acid, glufosinate ammonium, and deltamethrin) were spiked at the 5-fold fortification level relative to the DDVP concentration. The I585/I400 value for DDVP was much higher than those for the other pesticides (<0.1 except for 2,4-dicholorphynoxyacetic acid) indicating that the ratiometric fluoroprobe had a high selectivity for DDVP (Fig. 4D). Furthermore, a 3.5% reproducibility value was obtained based on the relative standard deviation (RSD) of five-parallel tests at a fortification level of 20 μg/L (Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: DDVP detection in real-world food samples

ce:section.ce:para: Herein, we designed and optimized a ratiometric fluoroprobe (also called a dual-emitting fluorescence sensor) for the sensitive, selective and reproducible detection of DDVP in food matrices. The fluoroprobe was based on the joint action of DDVP causing an inhibitory effect on AChE and the redox reaction between MnO2 NSs and TCh. Following the addition of DDVP, the FIs of okra-derived CQDs were substantially quenched, whereas those of AR were markedly enhanced due to the formation of AR-ox by MnO2 NSs. The MnO2 NSs-CQDs/AR based fluorescence sensor achieved several key performance metrics, including a wide linear range (4–120 μg/L), low LOD (1.2 μg/L), high selectivity, excellent stability and very good reproducibility. Moreover, the method produced good fortification recovery and high accuracy/precision for trace-level DDVP detection in real-world foods, thereby exhibiting excellent prospects for conventional monitoring applications.

He Mei: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiaolei Zhu: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ziqian Li: Formal analysis. Jiahui Jiang: Investigation. Huili Wang: Writing – review & editing. Xuedong Wang: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Peipei Zhou: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was jointly sponsored by the Natural Science Foundation of China (22076134), Key Science & Technology Project of Suzhou City (SS202028 and 2022SS24), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (LQ21B050004) and South Zhejiang Institute of Radiation Medicine and Nuclear Technology (ZFY-2022-K-004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137507.
